speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. You were talking about having met your husband and getting married in Sweden in 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We got married April 24th, 1949 and my brother got engaged, as I mentioned, [INAUDIBLE]. And the same summer, my brother got married and went to America. I had a visa to go to America, too, because we had a cousin that was-- had great means and he wanted us to come over. But I wanted to go to Israel, and my husband promised me that we are going to go to Israel. But this was a very difficult situation in Israel, at that point. The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Arab-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jews from the Arab countries had a priority to come to Israel, not I that lived peacefully in Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- the time went by-- it was already 1953-- and the situation in Israel was still very tough. I had a few-- I had two cousins, one from my mother's side, the only survivor from the seven brothers and sisters in all my cousins, and he told me not to come at that point. And I had another cousin in Haifa that was also one of the few cousins that survived from my father's family, also from seven brothers and sisters and all the children, and he was-- from a family of eight children, he was the only one that survived. And he also told me that this is not the time to come to Israel. Of course, I was sending them packages from Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was already 1953, and I finally decided to get pregnant. And we gave the world another chance, and I had my baby in May of 1953. And in December 1953, we came temporary to America. And I'm still temporary in America. I was 10 times to Israel, and I am a great supporter. And I always, sometimes think I wish I would made my life there, but I can not regret. When I first arrived from Sweden to America, I didn't like it so much. But I got used to the good things, and to some of the bad things. I have a wonderful family. I have two sons, and both of them are married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They each have three wonderful children. We just had an addition of twins last December.

speaker: interviewer
text: December of 1994?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest son got married 10 years later than my younger son. So my younger son has three children-- a 14-year-old boy, 11-year-old girl-- they are going to be 11 and 14 this summer-- and a three and 1/2-year-old little boy, Henry, and the other one is [INAUDIBLE], Benjamin. Joseph, which is my oldest son, named after my father, and named after my husband's father, [INAUDIBLE], has a son, Eric, four years old, and two children, a boy and a girl, Marissa and Jordan. So he ate some ketchup and he speeded up. So he has three children and Ronnie David, that this man named after the grandfather of my husband, also has three children. Ronald David has a wonderful, wonderful wife, Marianne. Both of my children, thank God, are very successful. They were in the-- they were computer nuts in the computer business when it just started. They are wonderful people-- charitable [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and sensitive. And they are millionaires, believe it or not. But like their grandfather, they say, the good lord gave me to distribute in a good way. And they are charitable and wonderful children. I am involved in the Holocaust Memorial Foundation. I am one of the founders, together with my husband. My wonderful children made a Lachman Foundation, and we endowed the library in the Holocaust-- the Lachman Library and Research Center in the Holocaust Foundation. I am presently in poor health. I have kidney failure, and I am on dialysis. Before that, I did a lot of speaking. Our organization, the Holocaust Foundation, was instrumental in bringing about a law whereby the Holocaust education is compulsory in the state of Illinois. And we-- we were the first state to have such a law. And it is good for the young people to learn from the past, to protect the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who of your family survived, of your father, mother, and brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: None of my family's brothers and sisters-- my parents-- both my father's seven brothers and sisters and my mother's seven brothers and sisters, none of them survived. From my maiden-- mother's family, just one cousin is living in Ramat HaSharon in Israel-- [INAUDIBLE] Gliker-- he survived from my mother's family. From my father's family, I have one cousin in Haifa, [INAUDIBLE] Strykowski, which is-- was my maiden name, and he survived. I have a cousin in Chicago, [? Felix ?] [? Dykman. ?] He's a son of a sister of my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] father. And I have a cousin in Florida, David Strykowski, that he also survived. He's a son of a brother of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother got sent-- it was a transport from my hometown-- to Treblinka, together with my grandmother. And my brother survived, with a miracle. As I mentioned, after I spoke on the telephone to the Jewish Congregation after I smuggled into Piotrków that I arrived, they listened in into the telephones and they arrested all of the people and shut them up next day. But they didn't succeed to get my brother because it-- he was at work that day. It was just a matter of luck. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The same German that my brother worked for the day that he wasn't shot, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was standing on the Umschlagplatz when they were deporting my hometown, and he was selecting the people. My mother and my grandmother went to the deportation. He recognized my brother and said, you are a good worker. You come to me. And my mother gave him-- he didn't want to go. He wanted to assist my mother. And my mother gave him a push and she said, go. That's how my brother survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to your hometown since the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Poland in 1988, and my older son went with us, my husband and I. We went-- this was a commemoration-- we went to Treblinka, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and had-- I had my cry there, [SOBBING] by the stones there. We went to Auschwitz, where the majority of my husband's family perished. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in the Warsaw Ghetto, and I commemorated my friends that perished. I went to Cracow, and commemorated my friends. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in Piotrków, in the town where I was caught. I was 30 kilometers from my hometown, and I didn't have the emotional strength to go to my hometown. Because the last thing I saw in my memory, and I still see, is when I walked out from my home, my mother was standing in the window. That window faced the whole street that I needed to walk down, and she was stayed at that window until I couldn't see her anymore. I had to turn in on [INAUDIBLE] street and smuggle out of that building. And this is the picture that I have in my mind. I couldn't go there and see somebody else living in my house, and see the town [INAUDIBLE] and nobody there. I couldn't go. So I didn't go to my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you like to give your children and grandchildren and future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, I have to say to my children, thank God, as I mentioned, are wonderful people and everything, and my son usually says, Mommy, what you went through gave-- give me-- gave me faith. It gave me strength to cope. I never burdened them. I never told them too many stories when they were young. But when they got older, they got the right books and I did want them to know about their roots and to know about life. But when they were little, I never bur-- What I want to give a message is that we are all stuck on one planet. We better care about one each other, or we are going to kill one each other. So there's not too many choices. The way the world is going, with the atomic power, if we are not going to control ourselves, we are going to all end up with nothing. I feel that there is room enough for everybody. We should better produce food, instead of ammunition, and maybe we are going to learn to tolerate each other, to live with each other, and to help each other, and it's about time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe in God now?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a power. There has to be a power. Because everything couldn't be so coincidental and fit so right and work so good. But I sometimes wonder where God was during the Holocaust. How could he let it be done to any people, let alone, as we sometimes feel, that we were chosen to hold and give the legacy of the 10 Commandments to the world, which is the greatest [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] humane code for humanity. If we would just live by the 10 commandments, it would be a wonderful world. It would be, really, a good world to live on, to care, to help, and to share. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My grandchildren, thank God, are going to Solomon [INAUDIBLE] Day School. And now, our older grandson that is going to be 14 this summer, Benjamin, that is really a wonderful child-- and I have to see-- say, that nobody has everything. Thank God we worked very hard, and we are independent. My children work very hard, and they were very fortunate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there are certain things you cannot buy for money. You cannot buy your health. You cannot buy your joy from your children. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I cannot buy my health, but I have a lot of joy from my family. I have a wonderful husband that, in spite of my disabilities-- my hands-- he's my hands. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He's my life, and I'm grateful that I have him. And I hope my children will stand by him when I am not going to be there. [SOBBING] And I hope they're going to be remember, which is most important, the care about one each other. I keep telling them, there is a whole world of strangers. They are just two brothers. So they have to care about each other, and care about the world around him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. We appreciate your sharing.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Excuse me that I got soft, but sometimes the good things are sometimes hard to talk about. I'm very grateful--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're grateful.

speaker: interviewee
text: In spite of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I say, I am grateful for the Holocaust Foundation that brings forth education, and we have to see to it to learn from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell me when. These are the pictures I carried in my rectum of my family. Very few survivors have pictures of their family. So I consider myself fortunate that I have them. All of the enlargements were made from that. This is my father, Joseph Strykowski, perished in Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in 1939, part of the group picture. This is my mother, Gitel Strykowska, picture taken in 1942 for Aryan papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was an enlargement from--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an enlargement of a picture that I rescued in my rectum. This is my picture, taken in 1938. I saved it just to realize how I looked then. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my brother and I in the summer place in 1959, in a cornfield. I saved it to realize where I came from. This is a picture of [INAUDIBLE], my group-- Zionist group-- taken in July, 1942 in the garden that I mentioned. And here I am above the cut out little picture of the sister of-- that I gave to my friend. From this picture, just two people survived, myself and Benjamin [INAUDIBLE], that is right here in the-- zoom in a little more. This is in the middle, the boy, over, that-- yeah, survived. And two of my best girlfriends joined the underground and succeeded. Put it down a little. There on the top. No, on the top. On the top, on the top. Yeah, right here. From that side is [INAUDIBLE] that got killed in the underground. A little more to the left. The left side, move in. Move in. More move in. And this is, above-- put it down a little-- is Helene [INAUDIBLE], my dearest girlfriend, and she also perished in the underground. And here is Itzak Rosenblatt, our [INAUDIBLE] leader. He had perfect Aryan looks, but he had a shorter leg, and he wasn't allowed to join the underground, nor to denounce us by his different looks.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Doric ?] [? Leiberskin ?], the leader from the [INAUDIBLE] and the leader from [INAUDIBLE],

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken before the war, right before the war. Gusta Davidson Dranger, one of the leaders from the underground. She was the one, together with her husband, [INAUDIBLE], they wrote the Chalutz [NON-ENGLISH] newsletter. She also wrote, in prison, on scraps of toilet paper, that we hope to have translated from Hebrew to English. It is in the Polish original.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [NON-ENGLISH] which is The Diary of Justina, written in Hebrew. But these are the original pictures of the scraps of toilet paper she wrote her diary in. This is Shimson-- [INAUDIBLE] Dranger. He was the husband of Justina, and also one of the leadership of the underground. All of the people I mentioned perished. Very few that reached the underground survive. This is-- This is my husband, Earl, our granddaughter, Julia, and I at a Bar Mitzvah, August, 1994. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is our younger son, Ronald David, his wife, Marianne, Benjamin, the Bar Mitzvah boy, and Julia, our granddaughter. August, '94.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the baby, Henry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is our older son, Joseph Isaac Lachman, his son, Eric, and his wife, Helene.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the newest addition to our family, our twins, Joey's children, Marissa and Jordan. They are presently a half a year old. They were born in December, '94. This is my hu-- This is my husband, Earl.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Judy. We are married 46 years. We have a wonderful life together.

speaker: interviewee
text: I appreciate the times we spend together, the good times and the difficult times, that he takes, now, care of me in a wonderful way in my sickness. We share our wonderful children, and our six grandchildren, that are our pride and joy and my miracle pills. I hope they are going to grow up in a world of peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: '94. So, he ate a little ketchup, my son. Caught up with his younger brother, Ronny, and he has three children and Ronald has three children. And those are my miracle pills, and I am proud of every one of them. And I hope they are going to be wonderful contributions to the human society.
